perturbation.original_text: Overdue payments shall accrue interest, at the lesser of 1 1/2% per month or the maximum allowable interest under applicable law, from due date until paid, and the owing party shall pay the owed party's costs of collection (including reasonable attorneys' fees).
perturbation.changed_text: Overdue payments shall accrue interest at a fixed rate of 2% per month, regardless of the maximum allowable interest under applicable law, from due date until paid, and the owing party shall pay the owed party's costs of collection (excluding attorneys' fees).
perturbation.explanation: By setting a fixed interest rate that disregards the 'maximum allowable interest under applicable law,' and excluding attorney's fees, the contract conflicts with usury laws and standard collection practices. This creates uncertainty in enforcement because a court may not enforce the 2% interest if it exceeds legal limits, and excluding attorney fees may not be allowed under certain state laws regarding debt collection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General usury laws and state laws governing debt collection practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts federal and potentially state usury laws, which set limits on the interest rates that can be charged on debts. The National Bank Act, 12 U.S.C. § 85, allows national banks to charge interest at the rate allowed by the state where the bank is located. Charging a fixed 2% interest rate without regard to such state limits could violate these laws. Also, excluding attorney fees could violate state debt collection practices depending on whether fees are statutory.

Rate of interest on loans, discounts and purchases Any association may take, receive, reserve, and charge on any loan or discount made, or upon any notes, bills of exchange, or other evidences of debt, interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, Territory, or District wh
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by disregarding the 'maximum allowable interest under applicable law' and fixing the interest rate at 2% per month. This directly contradicts usury laws as described in the Law Explanation, and aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 12 U.S.C. § 85, states that national banks can charge interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the state. By setting a fixed rate regardless of state laws, the modified contract violates this principle.

perturbation.original_text: Each party (the "Indemnifying Party") shall indemnify the other party (the "Indemnified Party") against any and all claims, losses, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees, which the Indemnified Party may incur as a result of claims in any form by third parties arising from: (x) the Indemnifying Party's acts, omissions or misrepresentations to the extent that the Indemnifying Party is deemed an agent of the Indemnified Party, or (y) the Indemnifying Party's breach of its privacy policy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party (the "Indemnifying Party") may indemnify the other party (the "Indemnified Party") against only direct claims, losses, costs and expenses, strictly excluding attorneys' fees, which the Indemnified Party may incur if found responsible as a result of claims in any form by third parties arising solely from: (x) the Indemnifying Party's fraudulent acts, omissions or intentional misrepresentations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates indemnification, including for attorney fees. The modification makes indemnification discretionary ('may'), limits it to direct claims, excludes attorney's fees and requires fraudulent intent. This weakens the protection provided to the 'Indemnified Party'. By limiting indemnification to only 'fraudulent acts', it introduces a high bar, potentially conflicting with standard indemnification practices under New York law and principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding indemnification, specifically regarding the scope and enforceability of indemnification clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text significantly limits the scope of indemnification, potentially contradicting standard practices and legal expectations regarding indemnification obligations. Especially, by excluding attorney's fees and requiring fraudulent acts, it deviates significantly from what a court may consider reasonable or enforceable under general contract law. Furthermore, NY General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 pertains to agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability. While this section doesn't directly prohibit the changes, by substantially reducing indemnification, the clause may be seen as an attempt to circumvent responsibility, leading to potential unenforceability issues.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated indemnification, including attorney's fees, while the changed text makes it discretionary, excludes attorney's fees, and requires fraudulent acts. This significantly weakens the protection for the 'Indemnified Party'. The provided law snippet, NY General Obligations Law § 5-322.1, addresses agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence in construction-related contracts. While the modified contract doesn't directly exempt a party from all liability, by substantially reducing the scope of indemnification to only 'fraudulent acts', it may be seen as an attempt to circumvent responsibility, potentially contradicting legal expectations. The change to 'may indemnify' and the exclusion of attorney's fees are key changes that create a contradiction, as they reduce the obligation where the original text had a mandatory requirement. The altered text would likely fail legal scrutiny as it undermines required protections.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without giving effect to conflict of laws principles. Both parties submit to personal jurisdiction in New York and further agree that any cause of action arising under this Agreement shall be brought in a court in New York City, NY.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be interpreted according to the parties' subjective intentions, irrespective of any state's laws or legal precedents. Any dispute arising from this agreement may be resolved through binding coin toss, the decision of which is final and non-appealable, and which occurs in San Diego, CA.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies New York law as governing and mandates dispute resolution in New York courts. The modification suggests that the parties' subjective intentions supersede established law. It replaces court resolution with a coin toss in a different location (San Diego). This directly contradicts standard choice-of-law and forum selection clauses, making the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of contract law regarding choice of law and forum selection; enforceability of arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts standard conflict of law principles and forum selection clauses. While parties can agree to arbitration, replacing it with a coin toss is unlikely to be enforced. 28 U.S.C. § 1391 dictates venue in federal courts, which implies a legal process beyond arbitrary methods like a coin toss. Additionally, the Federal Arbitration Act favors enforcing arbitration agreements, but this modified clause doesn't qualify as a legitimate arbitration agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement and general legal principles regarding dispute resolution and choice of law. The original text specified New York law and New York courts for dispute resolution, aligning with standard practice. The changed text abandons this in favor of subjective intentions and a coin toss in San Diego, CA. This conflicts with the principles outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1391, as evidenced by the scraped snippets, which establish the legal framework for venue in federal courts. A coin toss is not a legally recognized method of dispute resolution, and therefore creates a legal contradiction.
